title: Tiger and Sheep

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: 100 tigers and 1 sheep are on a magic island with only grass to eat.
rules: Tigers can eat grass, but they prefer to eat the sheep.
Only one tiger can eat the sheep at a time.
A tiger that eats the sheep BECOMES a sheep itself.
All tigers are perfectly rational. Their first priority is staying alive.
setup.question: Will the sheep be eaten?

learningObjectives: Apply backward induction to a self-elimination game.
Recognize when parity (odd vs. even count) determines the outcome.
Distinguish 'who can act' from 'who will act' under rationality.

stages.label: Comprehension
stages.prompt: If a tiger eats the sheep, what happens to that tiger?

options.text: It becomes a sheep, which the other tigers may then eat.

stages.explanation: The role-swap is the key rule. Eating the sheep is not a free meal -- it makes you the next target.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: Which technique will most directly resolve this problem?
stages.explanation: Each tiger's decision depends on what would happen in the resulting (n-1, sheep) world after the role-swap. That's a sequential reasoning problem -- backward induction.

stages.label: Ladder: n = 1 tiger
stages.prompt: With 1 tiger and 1 sheep, will the tiger eat the sheep?

options.text: Yes -- no other tigers, so no danger after the swap.
No -- the tiger refuses to risk becoming a sheep.

stages.explanation: After eating, the tiger becomes a sheep -- but there are no other tigers, so it lives. Free meal. The tiger eats.

stages.label: Ladder: n = 2 tigers
stages.prompt: With 2 tigers and 1 sheep, does the sheep get eaten?

options.text: No -- whichever tiger eats the sheep becomes a sheep, and the remaining tiger (in the n=1 world) will then eat it.

stages.explanation: If a tiger eats the sheep, we land in the n=1 case -- where the surviving tiger eats. So eating means dying. No tiger will go first. The sheep is safe.

stages.label: Ladder: n = 3 tigers
stages.prompt: With 3 tigers and 1 sheep, does the sheep get eaten?

options.text: Yes -- the eater lands in the n=2 world, where (from the previous step) the sheep is safe. So eating is survivable.

stages.explanation: If a tiger eats, the world becomes 2 tigers + 1 sheep -- and from n=2 we know that sheep is safe. So the eater survives as a sheep. Some tiger WILL eat. The sheep is NOT safe.

stages.label: Ladder: n = 4 tigers
stages.prompt: With 4 tigers and 1 sheep, does the sheep get eaten?

options.text: No -- the eater would land in the n=3 world, where the sheep gets eaten -- meaning the eater dies. No tiger will go.

stages.explanation: Eating now means becoming a sheep in a 3-tiger world, where the sheep gets eaten -- so eating means dying. No tiger eats. The sheep survives.

stages.label: Pattern
stages.template: When the number of tigers is __[a]__, the sheep gets eaten. When the number of tigers is __[b]__, the sheep is safe.
stages.explanation: Each rung flips the outcome: eating is safe iff the resulting world is unsafe for the new sheep, which alternates. So the answer depends entirely on parity.

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: With 100 tigers and 1 sheep, does the sheep get eaten?

options.text: No -- 100 is even, so the sheep is safe.

stages.explanation: 100 is even. The sheep is NOT eaten. Any tiger that ate would land in a 99-tiger world (odd), where the sheep gets eaten -- meaning the eater dies. So no tiger eats.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: In one sentence, what was the key insight that decided the answer?
How is this problem mechanically the same as Screwy Pirates? How does it differ?
If the rule were changed so that two tigers must eat together (and both become sheep), would the parity argument still work?

stages.label: Apply the parity rule
stages.prompt: Does the sheep get eaten when there are 50 tigers?

options.text: No -- 50 is even, so the sheep is safe.

stages.explanation: Even tiger count means eating is suicide for the eater, so no tiger eats.

setup.narrative: Same crew of 100 tigers, but now there are 3 sheep on the island. All other rules unchanged: a tiger that eats a sheep becomes a sheep itself.
setup.question: Will any sheep get eaten?

stages.label: Population accounting
stages.prompt: Starting from (100 tigers, 3 sheep), one tiger eats one sheep. What is the resulting population?

options.text: (99 tigers, 3 sheep) -- the eater becomes a sheep, replacing the one it ate (in the count).

stages.explanation: Tigers: 100 minus the eater = 99. Sheep: 3 minus the eaten one (= 2) plus the eater that became a sheep (= 3). So (99 tigers, 3 sheep).

stages.label: Will any tiger actually eat?
stages.prompt: A tiger will eat only if surviving as one of the sheep in the resulting world is safe. So the question becomes: in (99 tigers, 3 sheep), is the sheep population safe?

options.text: Yes -- in (99 tigers, 3 sheep) some tiger will eat (99 is odd), so the eater dies. The first tiger refuses.
No -- in (99 tigers, 3 sheep) no tiger will eat, so the eater survives. Some tiger DOES eat.
Trick question: the recursion still depends only on tiger parity. With 100 tigers (even), no tiger eats, so no sheep are touched.

stages.explanation: Adding more sheep doesn't change the recurrence on the eater's survival -- it only depends on whether the SUBSEQUENT world has an even or odd tiger count. So the parity rule from the original problem still applies: with T = 100 (even), no sheep gets eaten.
